sections.paragraph:
fut. יִדְלַק-

(1) to burn, to flame. (Aram. ܕܠܩܰ, id. To this corresponds Gr. δέρκομαι, prop. to flame, to shine, which is applied to the power of seeing; comp. שָׁזַף). Psa 7:14, חִצָּיו לְדֹלְקִים יִפְעַל “he makes his arrows flaming,” i.e. he shoots burning arrows. Followed by בְּ to set on fire, to kindle, Oba 1:18.

(2) The signification of burning is variously applied-(a), to the glow of love and friendship. Pro 26:23, שְׂפָתַיִם דֹּלְקִים “burning lips,” i.e. speeches which show or feign the warmest love.-(b) to anxiety, which is often compared to heat ( Isa 13:8 Psa 39:4). Psa 10:2. Comp. Schult. Ep. ad Menken. i.p. 49.-(c) to the heat of persecution, pursuing, whence דָּלַק אַחֲרֵי “to pursue hotly” (in the language of higher Germany, nachfeuern). Gen 31:36, כִּי דָֽקַלְתָּ אַחֲרַי “that thou pursuest me so hotly,” 1Sa 17:53. Followed by an acc. id. Lam 4:19, עַל־הֶהָרִים דְּלָקֻנוּ “they pursued us upon the mountains.” (Arab. دلق and ذلق have various figurative uses nearly approaching to those in Hebrew; as دلق to rush violently as a crowd, comp. letter c.; ذلق to be sharp and ready, as the tongue, comp. letter a.

Hiphil, to kindle, Eze 24:10, to heat, to inflame (used of wine). Isa 5:11, יַיִן יַדְלִיקֵם “wine inflames them.”
